_meta.notes: Five-beat arc for chorister-ascendant in Three Notches. The chorister leads the pre-climb chant — the line of voice that carries climbers up the switchback pass. Without the chant, the high wind takes them. Tonight's party includes a child on their first climb; the chorister's job is to keep the child's line in the chant through three notches and four hours. Win: voice>=10 AND harmony>=10 at turn>=8 (the line held; the party reached the third notch and bowed). Lose: harmony<=0 (a climber's line dropped; the wind found them; the chant did not hold).

beats.narrative: The pass square fills with the night's climbing party — six souls plus mules, plus you, plus the silence the cold pulls down from above the cloudline. The first notch is a switchback away. The line of voice that carries climbers up is yours to begin. The party will follow you. Whether they reach the top depends on whether the line holds.

suggestedVerbs.label: open the line
suggestedVerbs.description: take the first verse; the choir takes it after; the line begins

suggestedVerbs.label: lead the breath
suggestedVerbs.description: set the cadence the climbers' lungs will follow up the switchback

suggestedVerbs.label: carry the verse low
suggestedVerbs.description: begin in the lower register; the line will rise as the air thins

beats.narrative: A child of the half-dwarven family joins the climbing party — their first crossing. They have been practicing the bows but their voice is small and untrained, and the line they hold is thin. If the chant drops, theirs drops first. The other climbers have heard you before; the child has not. They will follow your line, not the choir's.

suggestedVerbs.label: lead for the child
suggestedVerbs.description: drop your line into a register their breath can match; the choir adjusts

suggestedVerbs.label: carry the child's line
suggestedVerbs.description: thread their thin line through yours; do not let it fall behind

suggestedVerbs.label: chant a children's verse
suggestedVerbs.description: the older verse for first climbers; the choir knows it; the child has heard it before

beats.narrative: Above the second notch the wind changes. It comes off the cloud-mass like an opinion — sharp, declarative, taking what is loose. The chant must hold against it. The choir behind you tightens; the child's line wavers; the older climbers find their second breath. The next half-hour decides whether the party crosses or turns back.

suggestedVerbs.label: chant against the wind
suggestedVerbs.description: the line steadies in the upper register; the wind takes what it must, the rest holds

suggestedVerbs.label: carry through the test
suggestedVerbs.description: the line bears down; the climbers feel it under their breath; the second notch passes

suggestedVerbs.label: lead the child's line
suggestedVerbs.description: the child's line into yours; the older choristers do the same below; the line thickens

fires.detail: cloudline wind cutting across the chant; line tested but holds

beats.narrative: The third notch is the highest and the hardest. The air is thin enough that the chant changes shape — short verses, longer rests between, the older line surfacing under the new. Every climber bows at the notch as they pass. The bow is part of the breath. The breath is part of the line. The line is yours.

suggestedVerbs.label: carry the older verse
suggestedVerbs.description: the wyrm-line surfaces under your chant; the climbers feel it without naming it; the bow lands cleanly

suggestedVerbs.label: rest between verses
suggestedVerbs.description: the high air gives back what the climber gives it; let the line breathe; the choir holds

suggestedVerbs.label: let the older line carry
suggestedVerbs.description: older than your training, older than the pass; the line that knows this notch carries you all

beats.narrative: The third bow lands. The party stands above the cloudline. The chant breaks into its final cadence — six voices, including the child's, including yours, including the choir's. The line is complete. The mules know they have arrived. Below, the cloud-mass moves; above, Highfield Ascending opens. The chorister's work is done for this climb.

suggestedVerbs.label: lead the final cadence
suggestedVerbs.description: the line resolves; the climbers' breath returns to their own keeping; the chant ends cleanly

suggestedVerbs.label: carry the line down
suggestedVerbs.description: the descent has its own verse; tomorrow's party will hear yours; the chant continues

suggestedVerbs.label: rest at the summit
suggestedVerbs.description: the line is complete; the breath is yours again; the climb is finished
